subject: Groin Hernias
title: Direct vs Indirect Inguinal Hernia
hook: One artery tells you which is which. Miss it and you lose the classic groin question.

points.heading: The inferior epigastric artery is the line
points.icon: an artery with a hernia neck crossing it
bullets: Indirect: neck lateral to the inferior epigastric artery.
Direct: neck medial to the inferior epigastric artery.
This landmark separates direct from indirect inguinal hernias.

points.heading: Indirect is congenital, direct is acquired
bullets: Indirect runs through a patent processus vaginalis sac.
Direct pushes straight through Hesselbach's triangle.
Direct is acquired, so more common in the elderly.

points.heading: Into the scrotum favours indirect
points.icon: a scrotum with a descending hernia
bullets: Descent into the scrotum is almost always indirect.
On coughing, indirect moves down the canal.
Direct bulges straight out near the external ring.

points.heading: Know the numbers
points.icon: an inguinal ligament with a marked deep ring
bullets: Inguinal hernias: about 75% of abdominal hernias.
Deep ring: ~1.25 cm above the mid-inguinal point.

captionLines: One artery tells you which is which.
Indirect sits lateral to the inferior epigastric artery and is congenital. Direct sits medial and is acquired.
Scrotal descent strongly favours indirect so use that landmark to nail the classic groin question.
